Q1:
You are invited to participate in a research study on Advice Seeking. You were selected to participate in this project because you are an adult over age 18. This study is sponsored by Boston College and the Alfred P. Sloan Foundation. 



The purpose of this study is to understand how we look for advice and guidance. This study will be conducted through this online survey. The survey should take you approximately 15 minutes to complete.



There are no direct benefits to you. However, you may feel gratified knowing that you helped further the scholarly work in this research area. You will be compensated approximately $2.50 for participating in this study. The exact  compensation amount will be determined by the average amount of time the survey takes across all participants, at a rate of $10/hour. There are no costs to you associated with your participation.

The Principal Investigator, Dr. Liane Young, and her research team at Boston College, will exert all reasonable efforts to keep your responses and your identity confidential. We may have access to, and may maintain in our data collection, your Prolific user ID. However, aside from your Prolific user ID, we will not maintain within our research data any information that uniquely identifies you, such as your name, location, or Internet Protocol (IP) address. In any report we publish, we will not include any information that will make it possible to identify a participant. Data that includes user-ID information will be collected and stored via the third-party server of Qualtrics. Data collected from the experiment will be coded to remove your name or any other personal identifiers. All records will be secured in a locked cabinet in our lab. The Institutional Review Board at Boston College and internal Boston College auditors may review the research records. State or federal laws or court orders may also require that information from research study records be released. Otherwise, the researchers will not release to others any information that could indicate your identity unless you give your permission, or unless the researchers become legally required to do so. Although the survey will not prompt you directly to identify yourself by name, email address or the like, the survey will include several demographic items that would prompt you to provide certain demographic information, such as your age, gender, ethnicity, education level and the like. In combination, responses to such questions could suggest your identity. Regardless, please know that the researchers will make no purposeful effort to discern your identity based on such information. Additionally, please note that you may opt to leave any such question blank. 


Your participation is voluntary. If you choose not to participate it will not affect your relations with Boston College. Some questions on the survey, such as comprehension questions, may be required in order to complete the survey and receive compensation. However, you may still choose to end your participation in the study at any time. If you have questions or concerns concerning this research you may contact the Principal Investigator at 617-552-0240 or liane.young@bc.edu. If you have questions about your rights as a research participant, you may contact the Office for Research Protections, Boston College, at 617-552-4778 or irb@bc.edu.

1. Honesty vs. kindness: Is it ever morally justifiable to tell a white lie to avoid hurting someone's feelings?
2. Self-defense: Is it morally acceptable to use physical force to defend oneself or others in a life-threatening situation?
3. Environmental ethics: Is it morally obligatory to prioritize environmental sustainability over economic growth?
4. Social justice: How can I determine whether a particular social justice movement or cause is morally justifiable?
5. Personal relationships: Is it morally acceptable to prioritize one's own happiness over the needs and feelings of others in a romantic relationship?

Cultural and Contextual Considerations
1. Cultural relativism: How can I navigate moral differences between cultures, and when is it morally justifiable to challenge cultural norms?
2. Historical context: How can I evaluate the morality of historical events or figures, considering the context in which they lived?
3. Power dynamics: How can I recognize and address power imbalances in relationships or social structures that impact moral decision-making?

Moral Education and Development
1. Moral development theories: What are the major theories of moral development, and how can they inform my own moral growth?
2. Moral education resources: Can you recommend resources (books, articles, courses) for learning about moral philosophy and ethics?
3. Reflecting on moral values: How can I engage in reflective practices to clarify and develop my own moral values and principles?
